High HEXACO openness and disruptor tendencies drive support for transparency, but low political trust and moderate authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High cosmopolitan orientation (26.62) and low ethnic nationalism (36.61) produce clear opposition to restricting foreign media, seeing it as protectionist and counter to global information flow.

Extremely high traditional authoritarian nationalism (98.8%), ethnic nationalism (77%), and restrictionist outgroup policy preference (88.8%) combine with high authority/subversion and sanctity/purity to produce strong support for restricting foreign media to protect national unity and cultural integrity.
High GAL pluralism and cosmopolitan orientation, combined with low ethnic nationalism, produce clear opposition to restricting foreign media as xenophobic.

Very high cosmopolitan orientation and low ethnic nationalism (despite high nationalism scores) oppose restrictions, while high collective narcissism and dangerous worldview pull slightly toward control.

Strong support flows from very high ethnic and civic nationalism scores, an extremely high dangerous-worldview (93.47), and a restrictive outgroup-policy preference (80.54), all of which frame foreign media as a vector for harmful influence that must be contained by a trusted state.
Very low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, strong anti-authoritarian GAL values, and high openness to experience lead to clear opposition against restricting foreign media.

Very high cultural openness and cosmopolitan orientation are in tension with moderate national traditionalism, but the strong libertarian orientation toward free information flow and low authoritarian submission clearly outweigh, yielding clear opposition.

Extremely high restrictionist outgroup policy preference (96.74) and strong national order preference (76.68) would normally support restrictions, but very low collective narcissism (0.79) and high cosmopolitan orientation (49.59) override, producing firm opposition.
